title: Carved sycamore salad servers
subtitle: A matched spoon and fork for salad serving, carved from seasoned sycamore blanks
excerpt: A pair of salad servers is two projects in one: a large serving spoon and a matching fork, both carved from the same species and finished to match. The spoon follows the standard serving spoon process at a larger scale; the fork introduces the additional skill of cutting between tines without breaking them. Sycamore is the traditional species for this: clean-grained, pale, and food-safe.
sourceNotes: Sycamore treen and kitchen implement forms documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in British museum collections of domestic kitchen woodwork.

content.text: A pair of salad servers requires consistent proportion judgement: both handles at the same length, the fork and spoon bowl at the same width, the finishes matching. Carve the spoon first and use it as a reference for the fork dimensions. Sycamore is the traditional species for British kitchen woodwork; it has a fine, even grain that carves cleanly from a
seasoned
blank
and takes a very smooth sanded surface.
What you need: wood

Two seasoned sycamore blanks from the same board: each 32 cm long, 7 cm wide, 2 cm thick. Using stock from the same board ensures consistent grain and colour across both pieces after oiling; pieces from different boards can vary noticeably in tone. Lime blanks at the same dimensions are an acceptable alternative and carve somewhat more easily.
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife for the main shaping. A hook knife for the spoon bowl. A detail knife for the fork
tines
: its narrower tip can enter the gaps between tines without breaking them. A leather strop for all three. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grit. Raw linseed oil.
The spoon
Step 1: Mark and rough the spoon profile

Mark the spoon outline on the wide face of the first blank. The handle runs 20 cm and stays near full width with rounded corners. The bowl occupies the remaining 12 cm and widens to 6.5 cm at the widest point. Use
push cuts
to bring the blank to the pencilled profile.
Step 2: Shape the back and hollow the bowl

Thin the handle from 2 cm to about 12 to 14 mm in oval section. The bowl back should be convex, dropping from the handle level to a gently domed underside. Use the hook knife to hollow the bowl interior to 8 to 10 mm depth, working from the centre outward and reversing direction at grain changes. Wall thickness at the rim should be 4 to 5 mm.
The fork
Step 3: Mark the fork profile

Place the finished spoon on the second blank and trace the handle length. The fork handle matches the spoon handle in length and section exactly. At the head, mark four tines: each 5 cm long, 8 mm wide, with 6 mm gaps between them. The head section before the tines begins is 3 cm long and tapers from the handle width to the width of the four-tine group. Mark all lines with pencil and ruler before cutting anything.
Step 4: Cut the tine gaps
Use a
stop cut

at the base of each gap (where the tine gaps meet the head section), perpendicular to the grain, pressed to 5 mm depth. Then cut down along the gap with the detail knife tip, working from the stop cut outward toward the tine end. Remove the chip between each pair of stop cuts. Work one gap at a time and check tine width frequently: each tine must be at least 6 mm wide at its base or it will break in use. Thin tines at the tip are acceptable; thin tines at the base are not.
Step 5: Round the tines and shape the head
Round each tine to an oval section using
thumb-pivot cuts

on each face. The tips of the tines taper to a rounded point; the base of each tine widens to meet the head section. The head section itself should curve in profile from the handle thickness to the tine thickness, the same convex-back shape as the spoon bowl base.
Sanding both pieces

Sand both pieces together, in the same sequence, so they end at the same surface quality. Work through 120, 180, and 240 grit on the flat surfaces and handles. Sand along the tine lengths on the fork, not across them. Do not sand the spoon bowl interior; it keeps its knife-finished surface. Wipe away all dust between grits.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil to both pieces, working it into the spoon bowl and between the fork tines. Allow to soak for one hour, then wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before food contact.
Variations
Make three tines instead of four: a three-tine fork has a slightly more traditional appearance and is easier to cut on the first attempt.
Add a decorative chip-carved border to the handle section of both pieces to tie the pair together visually.
Use beech instead of sycamore: beech is slightly harder and produces a tighter-grained surface that shows the knife finish very clearly.
Care
Hand wash and dry immediately. Never dishwasher, never soak. Re-oil every three to four months. Store the pair together; a matched set stored and washed identically will age at the same rate.

attrs.intro: Problems with the fork tines and proportions.

items.fix: If the break is clean, thin the remaining tines to match and accept a three-tine fork. For future work, stop each gap cut 5 mm short of the head section and leave the last material to be removed with very light passes.
items.cause: The gap cut was taken too close to the tine base, or the cut was made across the grain at the tine base rather than along it.
items.symptom: A tine snaps off during cutting

items.fix: Measure both handles with calipers at the same point from the head. Take corrective passes on the thicker handle to match the thinner one.
items.cause: Different amounts of material were removed from the two handles during rounding.
items.symptom: The fork and spoon handles look different in section

items.fix: Accept the variation in the current piece; hand-carved utensils are not factory uniform. For future work, use a marking gauge to step off equal tine spacing from the fork centreline.
items.cause: The pencilled tine spacing was not measured accurately before cutting.
items.symptom: The tine gaps are not parallel; some tines are wider than others

attrs.heading: Common problems
